items.title: IIS deployment basics for ASP.NET Core (in-process vs out-of-process)
items.description: ASP.NET Core apps run behind IIS through the ASP.NET Core Module (ANCM), which is installed by the .NET Hosting Bundle and acts as a reverse proxy/process manager. In the default in-process model ANCM hosts Kestrel inside the IIS worker process (w3wp.exe) for the lowest latency; in out-of-process mode IIS forwards requests over HTTP to a separately spawned dotnet/Kestrel process. The published output is deployed to a physical folder, an IIS website or application is pointed at it, and the app pool is set to 'No Managed Code' because the CLR is loaded by ANCM, not by IIS itself. The web.config in the publish output wires ANCM to the app and must be preserved during deployment.
items.applicability: Applies to any ASP.NET Core site served by IIS on Windows Server, including this platform's Web project when deployed to IIS rather than run via dotnet run.
items.example: Install the .NET 10 Hosting Bundle, run `dotnet publish -c Release -o C:\inetpub\LocalAIFactory`, create an IIS site pointing at that folder, and set its app pool to '.NET CLR Version = No Managed Code'. web.config contains `<aspNetCore processPath="dotnet" arguments=".\LocalAIFactory.Web.dll" hostingModel="inprocess" />`.
items.limitation: In-process hosting runs one app per worker process, so two apps cannot share an in-process app pool; the Hosting Bundle version must be at least as new as the app's target framework.

items.title: Windows Server roles and features required to host ASP.NET Core on IIS
items.description: Hosting ASP.NET Core on IIS requires the Web Server (IIS) role with a specific set of role services rather than a blanket install. Core services include Common HTTP Features (Static Content, Default Document, HTTP Errors), Health and Diagnostics (HTTP Logging, Request Monitor), Performance (Static/Dynamic Compression), and Security (Request Filtering plus Windows Authentication when integrated auth is used). The .NET Hosting Bundle supplies ANCM and the runtime; you do not need the legacy ASP.NET 4.x feature for a pure ASP.NET Core app. Installing only the needed role services keeps the attack surface minimal.
items.applicability: Applies when provisioning a fresh Windows Server (or Server Core) host that will serve this platform behind IIS, especially in regulated banking environments where minimal footprint is mandated.
items.example: `Install-WindowsFeature Web-Server, Web-Common-Http, Web-Http-Logging, Web-Stat-Compression, Web-Dyn-Compression, Web-Filtering, Web-Windows-Auth, Web-Mgmt-Console` then install the .NET Hosting Bundle and run `net stop was /y && net start w3svc` so IIS picks up ANCM.
items.limitation: Windows Authentication and management tooling are optional and should only be added when actually required; enabling unused role services widens the surface unnecessarily.

items.title: Hosting an ASP.NET Core app as a Windows Service
items.description: When a long-running .NET app needs to start with the machine without IIS in front of it (for example a worker or an internal API), it can be hosted as a Windows Service using the Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting.WindowsServices integration and a call to UseWindowsService() on the host builder. The service is registered with sc.exe or New-Service, runs under a dedicated service account, and the Service Control Manager handles start, stop, and automatic-restart-on-failure recovery. Kestrel still binds the listening ports directly since there is no IIS reverse proxy in this model. Content root must be set explicitly because a service's default working directory is the system folder, not the binary folder.
items.applicability: Use when a background or self-hosted component must survive logon/logoff and start at boot independently of IIS; less common for the interactive Web UI, which is typically IIS-hosted.
items.example: `sc.exe create LocalAIFactoryWorker binPath= "C:\apps\Worker\LocalAIFactory.Worker.exe" start= auto obj= ".\svcLAF" password= "<secret>"` followed by `sc.exe failure LocalAIFactoryWorker reset= 86400 actions= restart/5000/restart/10000/restart/30000`.
items.limitation: A Windows Service has no IIS request pipeline, so features like centralized HTTP logging, app-pool recycling, and integrated Windows auth must be handled in-app or are unavailable.

items.title: Scheduled tasks, app-pool identity, and service accounts
items.description: Each IIS app pool runs under an identity; the default ApplicationPoolIdentity is a per-pool virtual account (IIS APPPOOL\<PoolName>) that is the least-privilege choice for local resource access. For SQL Server with Windows Authentication or for cross-machine access, a dedicated domain service account (gMSA where supported) is preferred so credentials are managed centrally and rotated automatically. Maintenance jobs that must run on a clock — reindexing, log pruning, cache warm-up — belong in Windows Task Scheduler configured to 'Run whether user is logged on or not' under the same least-privilege service account, not in an interactive user context. Grant that account only the specific SQL and file-system rights it needs.
items.applicability: Applies to choosing identities for app pools, Windows Services, and Task Scheduler jobs that touch SQL Server, the Data Protection key ring, import folders, or log directories.
items.example: Map the app-pool virtual account into SQL: `CREATE LOGIN [IIS APPPOOL\LocalAIFactory] FROM WINDOWS; CREATE USER ... ; ALTER ROLE db_datareader/db_datawriter ADD MEMBER ...`. For nightly maintenance, register a task: `schtasks /Create /TN "LAF-Nightly" /RU "DOMAIN\svcLAF" /SC DAILY /ST 02:00 /TR "powershell -File C:\ops\nightly.ps1"`.
items.limitation: Group Managed Service Accounts require an Active Directory domain and KDS root key, and ApplicationPoolIdentity virtual accounts cannot authenticate to remote machines without additional Kerberos/constrained-delegation configuration.

items.title: Firewall rules for an IIS + SQL Server deployment (descriptive)
items.description: A correct firewall posture is deny-by-default with narrowly scoped allow rules for exactly the ports the deployment needs. Typical inbound rules permit HTTPS (TCP 443) to the IIS host from the client network and, when used internally, HTTP (TCP 80) only for redirect-to-HTTPS; the SQL Server engine (TCP 1433, or its configured static port) is allowed only from the application tier's address range, never the public network. Optional local-only services in this platform (Qdrant, Ollama) should be bound to loopback or restricted to the host and not exposed through the firewall at all. Rules should be scoped by remote IP/subnet, profile (Domain/Private), and protocol so the surface stays minimal.
items.applicability: Applies when documenting or reviewing the network exposure of the web tier and database tier; this item describes intended posture and is not an instruction to alter live firewall configuration.
items.example: Intended rule set, descriptively: allow inbound TCP 443 to the web host from the corporate subnet; allow inbound TCP 1433 to the SQL host only from the web host's address; keep 11434 (Ollama) and 6333 (Qdrant) closed externally and bound to localhost. All other inbound traffic remains blocked by the default-deny baseline.
items.limitation: This item is descriptive only and must not be read as guidance to disable Windows Firewall or weaken any security control; concrete rule changes require change-control approval and validation against the organization's network policy.

items.title: Health checks and readiness vs liveness endpoints
items.description: ASP.NET Core's health-check middleware exposes endpoints that load balancers, IIS Application Initialization, and monitoring agents probe to decide whether to route traffic. A liveness check answers 'is the process up and not deadlocked' and must stay cheap and dependency-free, while a readiness check answers 'can this instance serve real work' and may verify critical dependencies such as the SQL Server connection. Optional dependencies must degrade gracefully: in this platform Qdrant and Ollama are gated behind config flags and their absence should be reported as degraded, not unhealthy, because the system is designed to run MSSQL-only. Health probes must never trigger blocking synchronous calls on the request path; cached snapshots (the existing IServiceHealthCache pattern) are read instead.
items.applicability: Applies to exposing /health (liveness) and /health/ready (readiness) for IIS warm-up and external monitors, and to deciding which dependencies gate readiness.
items.example: Map two endpoints: `app.MapHealthChecks("/health/live", new() { Predicate = _ => false })` for liveness and `app.MapHealthChecks("/health/ready", new() { Predicate = c => c.Tags.Contains("ready") })`, tagging only the SQL Server check as 'ready' and reporting Qdrant/Ollama as Degraded when their flags are off.
items.limitation: Readiness that probes external services synchronously can itself cause hangs; never let a health endpoint depend on a live Qdrant/Ollama call, and keep liveness free of database access so a DB outage does not also kill liveness.

items.title: Log rotation and retention for IIS, ANCM, and application logs
items.description: Three log streams need management on an IIS-hosted .NET host: IIS request logs (W3C, under %SystemDrive%\inetpub\logs\LogFiles), ANCM stdout logs, and the application's own structured logs. ANCM stdout logging is intended for short-lived diagnostics and should be disabled in steady state because it writes unbounded files that fill the disk; enable it only to capture startup failures. Application logging should roll by day and by size with a bounded retention window and a total cap, and IIS logs should be pruned on a schedule because IIS does not delete them automatically. A documented retention period (balancing audit/forensic needs against disk and privacy constraints) governs how long each stream is kept.
items.applicability: Applies to any IIS-hosted deployment of this platform; especially important on SQL Express / small VMs where an unbounded log can exhaust the system drive and take the site down.
items.example: Set `<aspNetCore stdoutLogEnabled="false" stdoutLogFile=".\logs\stdout" />` except during incident triage; configure the file logger with daily rolling, a per-file size limit, and retainedFileCountLimit; schedule a task to delete IIS logs older than 90 days: `forfiles /p C:\inetpub\logs\LogFiles /s /m *.log /d -90 /c "cmd /c del @path"`.
items.limitation: Retention windows are policy-driven and may be constrained by audit requirements (which can mandate longer retention) or data-privacy rules (which can mandate shorter); leaving ANCM stdout logging enabled in production is a common cause of disk-full outages.

items.title: Backup jobs and restore drills for SQL Server and the key ring
items.description: Because MSSQL is the primary memory store for this platform, a defined backup strategy is mandatory: full backups on a schedule, differential backups between them, and frequent transaction-log backups when the database is in the FULL recovery model, sized to the recovery point objective (RPO). Backups must be stored off the database host and protected, and the Data Protection key ring (see the key-persistence item) must be backed up alongside the database because losing it invalidates encrypted payloads even after a successful DB restore. The cardinal rule is that a backup is unproven until it has been restored: scheduled restore drills to an isolated instance verify the media, the recovery procedure, and the actual recovery-time objective (RTO). Document and rehearse the end-to-end restore so it is muscle memory before an incident, not improvised during one.
items.applicability: Applies to protecting the SQL Server database(s) and associated secrets/key material for this platform, and to validating that recovery actually works within the agreed RTO/RPO.
items.example: Nightly `BACKUP DATABASE [LocalAIFactory] TO DISK='...full.bak' WITH CHECKSUM, COMPRESSION;` plus log backups every 15 minutes; weekly restore drill: `RESTORE DATABASE [LAF_DrillCopy] FROM DISK='...full.bak' WITH MOVE..., RECOVERY;` then run consistency checks and confirm the app starts against the copy, recording the elapsed RTO.
items.limitation: Restore drills must target an isolated/non-production instance to avoid overwriting live data, and backups without periodic integrity checks (CHECKSUM/RESTORE VERIFYONLY) can silently rot; an untested backup provides false assurance.

items.title: Environment configuration: local vs production
items.description: ASP.NET Core selects configuration by environment via the ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT variable, layering appsettings.json with appsettings.{Environment}.json and then environment variables and other providers that override files. Secrets and credentialed connection strings must never live in committed config: in development use User Secrets, and in production use environment variables or a git-ignored local override, consistent with this platform's rule that connection strings with credentials go in environment variables and API keys are encrypted at rest. Production should disable developer-only behaviors (the developer exception page, detailed errors) and enable production safeguards such as HTTPS redirection and HSTS. For IIS, environment variables can be set per app pool or via web.config <environmentVariables>, keeping local and production cleanly separated.
items.applicability: Applies to wiring the same codebase to a developer's machine and to a hardened production host without changing source, and to keeping secrets out of source control.
items.example: Local: `dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:Default" "..."` and ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development. Production web.config: `<aspNetCore ...><environmentVariables><environmentVariable name="ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT" value="Production" /></environmentVariables></aspNetCore>` with the connection string supplied via a machine environment variable, not appsettings.json.
items.limitation: Environment variables set in web.config are visible to anyone who can read the file, so the most sensitive secrets belong in a protected store; an incorrect or missing ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT silently falls back to Production and can mask configuration mistakes.

items.title: Data Protection key ring persistence across restarts and recycles
items.description: ASP.NET Core Data Protection underpins antiforgery tokens, authentication cookies, and any payloads the app encrypts; by default each app generates keys at startup and, under IIS, may store them transiently so they are lost on app-pool recycle, machine reboot, or across web-farm nodes. The symptom is users being logged out and 'unable to unprotect' / antiforgery failures after every recycle. The fix is to persist the key ring to a stable, shared location and protect it at rest — a file share or database accessible to all instances — and to give the app-pool identity read/write to that location. In this platform the key ring lives under a git-ignored keys/ folder and must be backed up with the database, because losing it makes existing encrypted data and API keys undecryptable even after a clean restore.
items.applicability: Applies to every IIS deployment and to any multi-instance/web-farm setup; mandatory wherever cookie auth, antiforgery, or app-level encryption is used and the app pool can recycle.
items.example: `services.AddDataProtection().PersistKeysToFileSystem(new DirectoryInfo(@"C:\keys\LocalAIFactory")).SetApplicationName("LocalAIFactory").ProtectKeysWithDpapiNG();` and grant `IIS APPPOOL\LocalAIFactory` modify rights on C:\keys. SetApplicationName must match across instances so they share one ring.
items.limitation: DPAPI-protected keys are tied to the machine/user and will not decrypt on a different host unless a portable protector (certificate) is used; the key directory and its backups are highly sensitive and must be access-controlled like any secret.

items.title: Monitoring and diagnostics for an IIS-hosted .NET app
items.description: Operational visibility combines platform metrics, application telemetry, and on-demand diagnostics. Windows Performance Monitor counters (Process\% Processor Time, .NET CLR Memory, Requests Queued, app-pool worker recycles) and the Windows Event Log (Application channel, where ANCM records startup failures with the configuring event source) cover the host and process layer. Structured application logs with correlation/request IDs — this platform's RequestTimingMiddleware logs '-> {path} started' and '<- {path} {status} in {ms} ms', flagging requests over one second — make slow or hung endpoints locatable: a 'started' line with no matching 'completed' line pinpoints a stall. For deep diagnosis, dotnet-trace, dotnet-counters, and dotnet-dump (or a w3wp memory dump) capture CPU, allocation, and hang data without attaching a debugger to production.
items.applicability: Applies to standing up baseline monitoring/alerting and to investigating incidents (high CPU, memory growth, request hangs) on an IIS-hosted instance of this platform.
items.example: Watch ANCM Event IDs in Event Viewer for startup failures; alert on app-pool recycle frequency and Requests Queued via PerfMon; on a hang, grep the log for a '-> {path} started' with no '<- {path} ... in {ms} ms' to find the stalled endpoint, then `dotnet-dump collect -p <w3wp pid>` and analyze offline.
items.limitation: Capturing a full process dump briefly pauses w3wp and can affect live requests, so schedule heavy diagnostics in a maintenance window or on a spare instance; PerfMon counters require the relevant feature/counters to be installed and the collector to be running before the incident.
